cities.name: Shimla
cities.overview: Shimla is the capital of Himachal Pradesh, perched at 2,206m in the Himalayan foothills. Famous for its colonial-era Mall Road, Victorian architecture, and panoramic Himalayan views, it served as the summer capital of British India from 1864 to 1947. Today it remains one of India's most beloved hill stations, blending nostalgia with a vibrant mountain culture.
cities.history: Captain Charles Pratt Kennedy built the first British residence here in 1822, sparking a transformation from a quiet Nepalese village to the nerve centre of the British Indian Empire. The Kalka–Shimla Railway (1903), a UNESCO World Heritage Site, winds through 102 tunnels and 864 bridges. Shimla also hosted the landmark India–Pakistan Shimla Agreement in 1972.

places.name: The Ridge & Mall Road
places.description: The beating heart of Shimla — a long open promenade offering panoramic views of snow-capped Himalayas, flanked by Victorian buildings, cafes, and the iconic Christ Church. Completely vehicle-free.
places.tips: Visit at sunset for golden views. The Ridge is the starting point for most walks around Shimla.

places.name: Jakhu Hill & Temple
places.description: The highest peak near Shimla at 2,455m, crowned by a 108-ft Hanuman statue and an ancient temple. Accessible by ropeway or a forested trek. Beware of playful monkeys.
places.tips: Take the ropeway up and trek down through the forest. Early morning gives the best Himalayan views.

places.name: Viceregal Lodge (Rashtrapati Niwas)
places.description: A stunning 1888 Elizabethan structure, former summer residence of the British Viceroy. Now the Indian Institute of Advanced Study, it offers guided tours of its grand halls, library, and manicured gardens.
places.tips: Guided tours run every hour. The gardens are beautiful in spring when flowers are in bloom.

places.name: Kufri
places.description: A winter wonderland just 16km from Shimla, famous for skiing, snow activities, horse riding, and the Himalayan Wildlife Zoo. In summer, lush meadows and valley views make it equally enchanting.
places.tips: Best visited Dec–Feb for snow. Hire a horse for the meadow ride for the full Kufri experience.

places.name: Christ Church
places.description: The second-oldest church in North India (1857), this Neo-Gothic structure is Shimla's most photographed building. Its stained-glass windows depicting Faith, Hope, Charity, and Patience are extraordinary.
places.tips: Visit early morning for stunning light through the stained glass. Attend Sunday service for a unique experience.

places.name: Kalka–Shimla Toy Train
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage railway — 96km narrow-gauge journey from Kalka to Shimla through 102 tunnels, 864 bridges, and 919 curves. One of the most scenic train rides in the world.
places.tips: Book via IRCTC in advance. Sit on the right side going up for the best valley views.

places.name: Chail
places.description: Created by Maharaja Bhupinder Singh as his exile alternative to Shimla. Home to the world's highest cricket ground (2,444m), a heritage palace hotel, and dense deodar forests.
places.tips: Overnight stay recommended. The Chail Palace hotel is expensive but worth it for the experience.

places.name: Tara Devi Temple
places.description: A 250-year-old hilltop temple dedicated to Goddess Tara Devi, offering a peaceful spiritual experience and sweeping views of the Shivalik range and Shimla valley.
places.tips: Visit during Navratri for the grand festival atmosphere. The hilltop views are wonderful at any time.

cities.name: Manali
cities.overview: Manali sits at the northern end of the Kullu Valley where the Beas River rushes through pine and deodar forests. A magnet for trekkers, honeymooners, and adventure seekers, it serves as the launchpad for expeditions to Lahaul, Spiti, Ladakh, and the Rohtang Pass.
cities.history: Named after Manu — the Hindu lawgiver said to have meditated here — Manali has ancient roots. Its modern character was shaped by Tibetan refugees who settled here after 1959, blending Buddhist culture with the valley's Hindu traditions. Old Manali village retains the original mountain charm beneath its tourist veneer.

nearest_railhead.name: Joginder Nagar
nearest_railhead.note: Most visitors drive from Chandigarh or Delhi

places.name: Rohtang Pass
places.description: At 3,978m, Rohtang is snow-covered even in June. The gateway to Lahaul and Spiti valleys, it offers snowfields, yak rides, and jaw-dropping mountain vistas. Permit required in advance.
places.tips: Book the mandatory NGT permit online 2 days in advance. Start early — traffic is brutal by 9am.

places.name: Solang Valley
places.description: The adventure capital of Manali — skiing in winter, zorbing, paragliding, and snowmobiling in season. The Atal Tunnel (2020) now connects Solang to Sissu in Lahaul year-round.
places.tips: Visit in January–March for the ski season. Summer offers paragliding and zorbing at reasonable prices.

places.name: Hadimba Devi Temple
places.description: A striking 1553 pagoda-style wooden temple dedicated to Goddess Hadimba, set in a cedar forest. One of the most architecturally unique temples in all of Himachal Pradesh.
places.tips: Visit early morning when the forest is quiet and the temple is uncrowded. Dhungri Van Vihar forest around it is beautiful.

places.name: Old Manali Village
places.description: The original village above Manali Mall Road — cobblestone lanes, apple orchards, quirky cafes, guesthouses, and the ancient Manu Rishi Temple. A world away from the tourist bustle below.
places.tips: Walk across the bridge over the Manalsu stream. The cafes here serve the best momos and apple cider in Manali.

places.name: Beas River Rafting
places.description: White-water rafting on the Beas River from Pirdi to Jhiri — Grade 2–4 rapids through beautiful mountain gorges. Perfect for first-timers and seasoned rafters alike.
places.tips: Best from May to July before monsoon. Book with a registered operator and always wear provided safety gear.

places.name: Naggar Castle
places.description: A 15th-century castle built in 'kathkuni' style (interlocking wood and stone) by Raja Sidh Singh. Now a heritage hotel with a museum and spectacular valley views. Nicholas Roerich's gallery is nearby.
places.tips: Combine with the Roerich Art Gallery next door. Staying a night here is a memorable experience.

cities.name: Dharamshala
cities.overview: Dharamshala is a twin city — lower Dharamshala is the administrative hub while upper McLeod Ganj is the seat of the Tibetan Government-in-Exile and home of the 14th Dalai Lama. The two together offer a unique blend of Tibetan Buddhism, colonial-era buildings, and Kangra Valley culture.
cities.history: Settled as a British military cantonment in 1849, Dharamshala became a refuge for Tibetan exiles after the 1959 uprising. The Dalai Lama chose McLeod Ganj as his permanent home, turning the small hill town into a global centre for Tibetan Buddhism, meditation, and yoga. The famous HPCA cricket stadium here is one of the most scenic in the world.

nearest_railhead.name: Pathankot
nearest_railhead.note: Narrow gauge to Kangra then taxi

places.name: McLeod Ganj
places.description: The Dalai Lama's home — a vibrant mix of Tibetan monasteries, bookshops, meditation centres, Tibetan cuisine, and mountain cafes. Every lane tells a story of exile, resilience, and spirituality.
places.tips: Attend a public teaching by the Dalai Lama if scheduled — register for free on the official website.

places.name: Tsuglagkhang Complex
places.description: The main Tibetan temple complex housing a 1.5m gold and silver statue of Shakyamuni Buddha. The Dalai Lama's residence, the Tibet Museum, and the Namgyal Monastery are all within the complex.
places.tips: Remove shoes before entering. Photography is allowed in most areas. Visit the Tibet Museum for powerful historical context.

places.name: Triund Trek
places.description: A stunning 9km trek through oak and rhododendron forests to a high meadow offering 360-degree views of the Dhauladhar range. Camping at Triund is a magical experience under star-filled skies.
places.tips: Carry enough water and snacks. Camping is allowed — carry or hire gear from McLeod Ganj. Snow closes trail Nov–Feb.

places.name: Kangra Fort
places.description: One of the oldest and largest forts in the Himalayas, dating back over 3,500 years. Built by the Katoch dynasty, it was long considered impregnable. Devastated by an 1905 earthquake, its ruins still command the valley impressively.
places.tips: Combine with a visit to the Kangra Art Museum nearby. Hire a local guide for the full historical narrative.

places.name: Dal Lake
places.description: A serene Himalayan lake (not to be confused with Srinagar's Dal Lake) surrounded by deodar cedars and oak trees. Ideal for a quiet morning walk, birdwatching, or a picnic.
places.tips: Visit at sunrise for misty reflections. Avoid weekends if you prefer solitude. Boating available for a small fee.

places.name: Bhagsunag Waterfall
places.description: A beautiful twin-cascade waterfall reached by an easy 20-minute walk from McLeod Ganj through the Bhagsu village. The natural pool at the base is refreshing and the surrounding cafe scene is vibrant.
places.tips: Combine with the Shiva Cafe above the falls for chai with the best view. Slippery in monsoon — wear good shoes.

cities.name: Spiti Valley
cities.overview: Spiti is a high-altitude cold desert valley bordering Tibet, with a stark, moon-like landscape unlike anywhere else in India. Its ancient Buddhist monasteries perched on impossible cliffs, crystal rivers, and unpolluted night skies attract serious travellers willing to go off the beaten path.
cities.history: Spiti means 'The Middle Land' in Tibetan — historically situated between India and Tibet. The Key Monastery (founded ~1000 AD) and Tabo Monastery (996 AD) are among the oldest surviving monasteries in the Himalayas. Roads only became accessible in the 1990s; before that, Spiti was one of the most isolated regions in the world.

nearest_railhead.name: Shimla
nearest_railhead.note: Drive via Kinnaur or via Manali (summer only)

places.name: Key Monastery (Ki Gompa)
places.description: The largest and most famous monastery in Spiti at 4,166m, a fortress-like structure that has housed Buddhist monks for over a thousand years. Contains rare thangkas, ancient manuscripts, and stunning murals.
places.tips: Attend the morning prayers (6–7am) for an unforgettable experience. Respectful dress required. Overnight stay at the monastery guesthouse is possible.

places.name: Chandratal Lake
places.description: A crescent-shaped glacial lake at 4,300m, one of India's most beautiful high-altitude lakes. The turquoise water changes colour with the shifting light — from emerald to sapphire to deep indigo.
places.tips: Camping near Chandratal is magical. Carry all supplies — no shops nearby. Acclimatize in Kaza before visiting.

places.name: Kibber Village
places.description: One of the world's highest villages connected by a motorable road at 4,205m. A true high-altitude Tibetan village with whitewashed homes, prayer flags, and views of the snow-capped Himalayas.
places.tips: The Kibber Wildlife Sanctuary here is one of the best places in India to spot Snow Leopards (Nov–Feb).

places.name: Tabo Monastery
places.description: Founded in 996 AD, Tabo is called the 'Ajanta of the Himalayas' for its spectacular frescoes and stucco sculptures. One of the oldest continuously operating Buddhist monasteries in the world.
places.tips: The cave monasteries behind Tabo are worth exploring. Visit the main assembly hall for the most breathtaking ancient art.

places.name: Pin Valley National Park
places.description: A cold desert national park — one of the last refuges of the Snow Leopard in India. Also home to Siberian ibex, Himalayan wolf, red fox, and over 100 bird species including the rare snowcock.
places.tips: Hire a local guide from Mud village for wildlife spotting. Snow Leopard sightings are most likely in winter.

places.name: Dhankar Fort & Monastery
places.description: A precariously perched fort and monastery on a sheer cliff above the confluence of the Spiti and Pin rivers — one of the most dramatic locations in all of Himachal. The short hike to Dhankar Lake above it is breathtaking.
places.tips: The 1km hike up to Dhankar Lake is worth every step. Go in the morning before clouds roll in.

cities.name: Kasauli
cities.overview: Kasauli is a small, quiet cantonment town with wide pedestrian lanes, Victorian-era bungalows draped in bougainvillea, and long views across the Shivalik hills to the plains of Punjab. Far less crowded than Shimla, it's the ideal destination for those seeking unhurried mountain life.
cities.history: Established in 1842 as a British military cantonment, Kasauli retains its colonial character more authentically than almost any other hill station in India. The Kasauli Brewery (1820) — established by Edward Abraham Dyer — is the oldest working brewery in Asia and the birthplace of Murree Beer.

places.name: Monkey Point
places.open_hours: 7:00 AM – 5:00 PM (IAF controlled area — ID required)
places.description: The highest point in Kasauli offering a 360-degree panorama from the Himalayas to the Punjab plains. A small Hanuman temple sits at the top. Controlled by the Indian Air Force — carry government ID.
places.tips: Carry a valid photo ID — army personnel check at the entry gate. Go on clear days for the full Himalayan panorama.

places.name: Christ Church Kasauli
places.description: A beautifully preserved 19th-century Gothic church — one of the finest examples of colonial ecclesiastical architecture in Himachal Pradesh. The interior woodwork and stained glass are especially fine.
places.tips: Visit during the Sunday morning service for a unique experience. The church garden is perfect for quiet reflection.

places.name: Kasauli Brewery
places.description: The oldest working brewery in Asia (1820), established by Edward Dyer. Still producing whisky and rum under the Mohan Meakin brand. Guided tours available with prior appointment.
places.tips: Apply for a visit permit in advance through Mohan Meakin's office. A fascinating piece of living colonial history.

places.name: Kasauli Mall Road (Lower & Upper)
places.description: A peaceful colonial promenade through pine and horse chestnut trees with sweeping valley vistas. Quieter and more authentic than Shimla's Mall Road, lined with old bungalows, bakeries, and bookshops.
places.tips: Walk the Upper Mall towards the sunset point in the evening. The Lower Mall has better shops and bakeries.

places.name: Sunset Point
places.description: A famous vantage point where you can watch the sun dip dramatically behind the Shivalik hills, casting golden light across the valley. One of the most peaceful sunsets in the region.
places.tips: Arrive 30 minutes before sunset to get a good spot. The walk up is lined with wildflowers in spring.

places.name: Timber Trail Ropeway
places.description: Asia's longest ropeway at 1.8km, running over dense Shivalik forests near Parwanoo. The top station offers a resort with restaurants and valley views.
places.tips: Go on clear mornings for best visibility. The resort at the top has a good restaurant for lunch.

cities.name: Dalhousie
cities.overview: Dalhousie is a charming hill station spread across five hills — Kathalagh, Portreyn, Terah, Bakrota, and Balun. Named after Lord Dalhousie, who developed it as a British summer retreat, it retains Victorian and Scottish character in its churches, bungalows, and colonial lanes.
cities.history: Established in 1854 by Lord Dalhousie as a sanatorium for British troops, the town saw the great poet Rabindranath Tagore, freedom fighter Subhas Chandra Bose, and Netaji plan parts of the Indian independence movement here. The nearby Chamba Valley has an unbroken Hindu cultural heritage of over 1,000 years.

nearest_railhead.name: Pathankot
nearest_railhead.note: Taxi from Pathankot is the most common route

places.name: Khajjiar — Mini Switzerland
places.description: A lush circular meadow surrounded by dense deodar forests with a small lake in the centre — so reminiscent of Switzerland that the Swiss Embassy erected a signboard declaring it 'Mini Switzerland of India'.
places.tips: Visit on weekdays for fewer crowds. Paragliding and zorbing are available. The drive through deodar forest is as beautiful as the destination.

places.name: Dainkund Peak
places.description: The highest peak near Dalhousie at 2,745m, also called the 'Singing Hill'. A scenic 2km trek from Pholani Mata temple leads to the summit with stunning 360° panoramic views.
places.tips: Start before 8am to avoid afternoon clouds. The hilltop is an IAF area — carry ID. Views extend to Pakistan on clear days.

places.name: Satdhara Falls
places.description: Seven natural springs converge into a single beautiful waterfall surrounded by rhododendron trees. The water is said to have medicinal properties — local legend holds it cures skin ailments.
places.tips: Best visited during and just after monsoon when the falls are at full flow. Combine with the Subhash Baoli nearby.

places.name: Chamba Town
places.description: An ancient royal town on the Ravi River with a 1,000-year unbroken heritage. Known for its unique Chamba rumal (embroidered handkerchief art), Lakshmi Narayan temple complex, and the Bhuri Singh Museum.
places.tips: The Minjar Fair (August) is one of Himachal's most colourful festivals — plan around it if possible.

cities.name: Kullu
cities.overview: Kullu is a broad, open valley through which the Beas River flows, flanked by apple orchards, pine forests, and ancient temples. Known worldwide for its hand-woven shawls, the Kullu Dussehra festival, and river rafting, it forms an inseparable cultural twin with its famous neighbour Manali.
cities.history: The Kullu Valley was historically known as 'Kulanthapitha' — the End of the Habitable World. It has been a pilgrimage route for millennia, with over 360 temples dedicated to local deities (devtas). The annual Dussehra festival, held in October, draws over 200 devtas from across the valley in a unique celebration unlike anywhere else in India.

nearest_railhead.name: Joginder Nagar
nearest_railhead.note: Most visitors travel by road from Chandigarh

places.name: Dhalpur Maidan (Dussehra Ground)
places.description: The grand open ground where the internationally famous Kullu Dussehra is celebrated for 7 days every October. Over 200 devtas (deity palanquins) from across the valley converge here in a spectacular procession.
places.tips: Plan your trip around October for the festival. It starts the day after Vijayadasami — check the exact dates each year.

places.name: Raghunath Temple
places.description: The patron deity temple of the Kullu Valley and centrepiece of the Dussehra festival. The Raghunath idol was brought from Ayodhya by Raja Jagat Singh in 1651, giving Kullu its divine character.
places.tips: The temple is busiest during Dussehra. Respectful attire required — cover head and remove shoes.

places.name: Great Himalayan National Park
places.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site (2014) covering over 1,700 sq km of pristine Western Himalayan ecosystems. Home to the Snow Leopard, Himalayan brown bear, blue sheep, and over 375 bird species.
places.tips: Hire a registered guide from the park office in Shamshi. Multi-day treks into the park core zone require special permits.

cities.name: Lahaul Valley
cities.overview: Lahaul Valley is a long, narrow valley carved by the Chandra and Bhaga rivers, surrounded by glaciers and high mountain passes. Less visited than Spiti, it offers a raw, authentic Himalayan experience with Buddhist monasteries, ancient fortresses, and an uncluttered landscape.
cities.history: Lahaul was part of the ancient trade route between India and Central Asia. Historically ruled by the kings of Kullu and later Tibet, it became part of Himachal Pradesh in 1966. The Atal Tunnel (2020) — the world's longest highway tunnel above 10,000 feet — now connects it to Manali year-round.

nearest_railhead.name: Joginder Nagar
nearest_railhead.note: Drive via Manali through Atal Tunnel

places.name: Keylong (District HQ)
places.description: The district headquarters of Lahaul & Spiti at 3,100m — a quiet, pleasant town on the Bhaga River with Buddhist monasteries, apple gardens, and views of the Mulkila glacier.
places.tips: Good base for exploring Lahaul. The Kardang Monastery above Keylong is a 2-hour hike and well worth it.

places.name: Atal Tunnel (Rohtang Tunnel)
places.description: The world's longest highway tunnel above 10,000 feet at 9.02km, opened in 2020. It connects Manali to Lahaul bypassing the Rohtang Pass, keeping the valley accessible year-round for the first time in history.
places.tips: Drive through and stop at the north portal in Sissu for the stunning valley and waterfall view. The tunnel is well-lit and takes about 15 minutes.

places.name: Suraj Tal (Suraj Lake)
places.description: The third-highest lake in India at 4,950m near the Baralacha La pass on the Manali–Leh highway. A stunning glacial lake fed by snowmelt — one of the sources of the Bhaga River.
places.tips: Only accessible on the Manali–Leh route. Acclimatize well before visiting. Do not underestimate altitude — carry oxygen.

cities.name: Kinnaur Valley
cities.overview: Kinnaur is a dramatic valley carved by the Sutlej River, where Hindu and Buddhist cultures meet amid apple orchards and towering peaks. The Hindustan–Tibet Highway runs through it, offering one of the most spectacular drives in Asia with sheer cliff roads, hanging valleys, and ancient monasteries.
cities.history: Kinnaur has been a part of many kingdoms — from the Kullu kings to the Bushahr dynasty. The Hindustan–Tibet Road through Kinnaur was one of the historic trade routes to Tibet. Kinnaur's people are believed to be descendants of the mythological Kinners (divine beings), giving the valley its mystical identity.

nearest_railhead.name: Shimla
nearest_railhead.note: Long but scenic NH-5 drive along the Sutlej River

places.name: Sangla Valley
places.description: One of the most beautiful valleys in the Himalayas — the Baspa River valley flanked by dense apple orchards, silver birch forests, and the towering Kinnaur Kailash range. Chitkul village at its head is the last village before Tibet.
places.tips: Chitkul is the last Indian village on the Indo-Tibet border — carry your ID. The apple season (Sep–Oct) is magical.

places.name: Kinnaur Kailash
places.description: A sacred peak (6,500m) revered by both Hindus and Buddhists — believed to be Lord Shiva's winter home. A 79km parikrama (circumambulation) trek is done by pilgrims each year after Sawan.
places.tips: The view from Kalpa village is one of the most dramatic in Himachal — the peak turns golden-pink at sunrise.

places.name: Kalpa Village
places.description: A beautiful old village at 2,960m with apple orchards, traditional Kinnauri wooden architecture, and the most stunning face-on view of the Kinnaur Kailash peak. Home to the ancient Hu-Bu-Lan-Kar monastery.
places.tips: Stay at least one night for the sunrise over Kinnaur Kailash — it is unforgettable. Most guesthouses have rooftop views.

cities.name: McLeod Ganj
cities.overview: McLeod Ganj is the upper section of Dharamshala — a bustling, soulful town that serves as the seat of the Tibetan Government-in-Exile and residence of the 14th Dalai Lama. It draws pilgrims, backpackers, yoga practitioners, and meditation seekers from across the world.
cities.history: After the 1959 Tibetan uprising and the Dalai Lama's escape from Lhasa, the Indian government offered Dharamshala as a sanctuary. The Dalai Lama established his government-in-exile here in 1960. Today McLeod Ganj is called 'Little Lhasa' for its thriving Tibetan community, culture, and institutions.

nearest_railhead.name: Pathankot
nearest_railhead.note: Shared taxis and buses connect Pathankot to McLeod Ganj

places.name: Namgyal Monastery
places.description: The personal monastery of the Dalai Lama and the largest Tibetan monastery outside Tibet. Monks can be seen debating in the courtyard — a powerful and meditative sight.
places.tips: Arrive at 6am for morning prayers — one of the most moving experiences in McLeod Ganj. Photography inside the temple is restricted.

places.name: Tibet Museum
places.description: A deeply moving museum documenting Tibet's history, the 1959 uprising, the Dalai Lama's flight, and the life of Tibetan refugees. Essential for understanding the spirit of McLeod Ganj.
places.tips: Allow 90 minutes minimum. The personal testimonies section is the most powerful. Free entry — donations welcome.

places.name: Naddi Village & Viewpoint
places.description: A quiet village 5km from McLeod Ganj with some of the best unobstructed views of the Dhauladhar range and Kangra Valley. Far fewer tourists than the main town.
places.tips: Walk or take an auto from McLeod Ganj. Sunrise and sunset views are exceptional. The local restaurants here serve good food.

cities.name: Palampur
cities.overview: Palampur is Himachal Pradesh's tea capital — rolling green tea gardens against the dramatic backdrop of the Dhauladhar range create a landscape unlike anywhere else in the state. A lush, tranquil alternative to busier hill stations.
cities.history: Tea cultivation was introduced to Palampur by Dr. Jameson of the Bengal Medical Service in 1849, making the Kangra Valley one of India's oldest tea-growing regions. The area was under various dynasties including the Katoch clan of Kangra before British rule.

nearest_railhead.name: Baijnath–Paprola
nearest_railhead.note: On the Kangra Valley narrow-gauge railway from Pathankot

places.name: Kangra Tea Gardens
places.description: Lush rolling tea estates producing the famous Kangra Tea — a delicate, floral green tea known since 1852. Walk through the bushes, watch the plucking process, and taste freshly brewed Kangra tea at estate cafes.
places.tips: The Wah Tea Estate offers excellent guided tours. Spring flush season (March–April) is the most scenic and aromatic time to visit.

places.name: Baijnath Shiva Temple
places.description: A 1,300-year-old Shikara-style temple dedicated to Vaidyanath (Shiva as the healer). One of the finest examples of early medieval Pratihara architecture in the Himalayas. The Binwa River flows beside it.
places.tips: The Maha Shivratri fair here is spectacular. Visit early morning for the quietest and most spiritual experience.

places.name: Saurabh Van Vihar
places.description: A peaceful forest park named after Captain Saurabh Kalia (Kargil War martyr) with walking trails through rhododendron and cedar trees, and stunning views of the Dhauladhar range.
places.tips: Best in spring when rhododendrons bloom red. A popular spot for morning walks and picnics among locals.

cities.name: Mandi
cities.overview: Mandi is the commercial and cultural gateway to the upper Himalayas, situated where the Beas River widens into a valley. With over 81 ancient temples within its borders and a remarkable Shivaratri fair, it earned the title 'Chhota Kashi' (Little Varanasi).
cities.history: Mandi was the capital of the princely state of Mandi for over 500 years, founded by Rajas who were followers of Lord Shiva. The town's extraordinary density of 16th–18th century stone temples is unmatched anywhere in Himachal Pradesh.

nearest_railhead.name: Joginder Nagar
nearest_railhead.note: On the narrow-gauge Kangra Valley Railway

places.name: Trilokinath & Panchvaktra Temple
places.description: Panchvaktra Temple at the Beas–Uhl confluence is Mandi's most iconic structure — a 16th-century Shikhara-style temple with five faces of Shiva. Trilokinath temple nearby is equally ancient and revered.
places.tips: Best visited during the International Mandi Shivaratri Fair (February–March) when 81 devtas from across Mandi arrive in a spectacular procession.

places.name: Rewalsar Lake
places.description: A sacred lake revered by Hindus, Buddhists, and Sikhs alike — one of the rare sites of shared religious harmony in India. The floating reed islands on the lake are a unique natural phenomenon.
places.tips: Each faith has its own temple or gurudwara on the lakeside. Visit in morning for quiet reflection before tourist crowds arrive.

cities.name: Narkanda
cities.overview: Narkanda is a quiet apple-growing hill town on the old Hindustan–Tibet Road, surrounded by dense forests of oak, rhododendron, and Himalayan pine. It has Himachal's oldest ski slopes and some of the finest apple and cherry orchards in the state.
cities.history: Narkanda has been on the historic Hindustan–Tibet trade route since ancient times. British administrators used it as a transit camp. The ski slopes here were developed in the 1960s by the Himachal Pradesh Tourism Department — the oldest such facility in the state.

places.name: Hatu Peak
places.description: The highest peak near Narkanda at 3,400m, accessible by a 4km trek or a jeep road. The summit offers views of Kinnaur Kailash, the Sutlej Valley, and on clear days, peaks in Tibet.
places.tips: Go in clear weather only — summit clouds can block all views. The Hatu Mata temple at the top is an added attraction.

places.name: Narkanda Ski Slopes
places.description: Himachal Pradesh's oldest ski resort with natural snow slopes from January to March. HP Tourism offers ski equipment rental and basic beginner courses. Less crowded than Solang Valley.
places.tips: Book HP Tourism's ski packages in advance (November–December). Equipment rental available on-site but limited.

cities.name: Bir Billing
cities.overview: Bir is the landing site and Billing (2,400m above) is the take-off site for paragliding — together they form the paragliding capital of Asia and one of the top 5 sites in the world. Beyond paragliding, Bir is a Tibetan colony town with monasteries, meditation centres, and a laid-back cafe culture.
cities.history: Bir was established as a Tibetan refugee settlement in the 1960s, giving it a strong Tibetan Buddhist character with multiple monasteries and a thriving crafts community. The Billing plateau was discovered as a world-class paragliding site in the 1980s. The Paragliding World Cup has been held here multiple times.

places.name: Paragliding (Billing to Bir)
places.description: The iconic tandem paraglide from Billing plateau (2,400m) to Bir landing site — a 30-minute flight over Kangra Valley and Dhauladhar peaks. Ranked among the world's top 5 paragliding sites.
places.tips: Book only with licensed operators registered with HPPTDC. Morning flights have the most stable thermals. Check weather before booking.

places.name: Sherabling Monastery
places.description: A beautiful and active Kagyu tradition Buddhist monastery with a towering gold Buddha statue, colourful murals, and a peaceful retreat atmosphere. One of the most significant Tibetan monasteries in Himachal.
places.tips: Morning prayer at 7am is open to visitors. The monastery also runs meditation retreats — check their website for schedules.

places.name: Bir Tibetan Colony
places.description: Wander through the original Tibetan refugee colony with its carpet weaving workshops, butter tea shops, prayer wheel alleys, and authentic Tibetan restaurants. A small but vibrant piece of Tibet in India.
places.tips: Buy a hand-knotted Tibetan carpet directly from the weavers — far cheaper than buying from shops in Delhi or Chandigarh.
